head.ce:title: Reusable carbon dot/chitin nanocrystal hybrid sorbent for the selective detection and removal of Cr(VI) and Co(II) ions from wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The last few decades have seen a dramatic increase in heavy metal ions in industrial wastewater, especially Cr6+ and Co2+ ions, which have been shown to adversely affect human health; thus, there is a great need for their removal. Existing removal methods are time-consuming, expensive, and require high technical skills; therefore, there exists a need for a quick, simple, and cost-effective method. Chitin was selected from natural materials (shrimp waste), and an inexpensive, biodegradable, fluorescent carbon dot/chitin nanocrystal (C-dot/ChNC) sorbent for the simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater was synthesized from it. C-dot/ChNC shows high selectivity and sensitivity to Cr6+ and Co2+ ions and has high chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) adsorption capacities. These ions are weakly adsorbed on the C-dot/ChNC surface and are easily removed during regeneration, showing high cyclability. Thus, C-dot/ChNC is a viable sorbent for the removal of heavy metal ions during wastewater treatment.

An optically active carbon dot/chitin nanocrystal hybrid sorbent for the simultaneous detection and removal of chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) heavy metal ions from wastewater is presented. Isotherm studies show that adsorption is weak and chemical in nature and this advanced functional material has high adsorption capacity for these ions. The hybrid also exhibits high cyclability.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid population growth, industrialization, and urbanization have led to an increase in the amount of heavy metal ions in the environment. Heavy metals occur in nature as a result of volcanic activity and rock weathering; however, they are also introduced to the environment, especially aquatic ecosystems, through numerous industrial activities (Maleki, Hajizadeh, Sharifi, & Emdadi, 2019; Theydan, 2018). Toxic heavy metal ions are considered very harmful pollutants because they cannot be degraded by natural factors (Lu et al., 2012; Tong, Li, Yuan, & Xu, 2011), and can enter the human body via inhalation, the skin, or the gastrointestinal tract, posing a serious threat to human health (Begum, Devanna, Chandra, & Sultana, 2018; Witkowska, Słowik, & Chilicka, 2021). Due to these adverse effects, the detection and removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater is critical and, as such, has attracted the interest of many researchers (Bansod et al., 2017a; Fu & Wang, 2011; Khan & Saleem, 2018). Several approaches have been developed, some focused on the detection of heavy metal ions and others on their removal from wastewater (Bansod et al., 2017b; Fu & Wang, 2011); however, these methods tend to be time-consuming, expensive, and require high technical skills (Malik, Bashir, Qureashi, & Pandith, 2019). Therefore, there exists a need for a quick, simple, and cost-effective method for the simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater.

Chromium and cobalt are known to have particularly adverse effects on human health. Chromium exists in two forms: chromium(III) and chromium(VI). At low concentrations, chromium(III) is a relatively harmless micronutrient that plays a positive role in human nutrition and is vital for wellbeing; it is a nutritional supplement to glucose and lipid metabolism and plays a major role in maintaining blood glucose levels (Swaroop et al., 2019). However, chromium(VI) is a harmful heavy metal pollutant found in industrial effluent (Fenti, Chianese, Iovino, Musmarra, & Salvestrini, 2020; Mikhaylov, Maslennikova, Krivoshapkina, & Tropnikov, 2018) and can cause blood, nerve, and bone damage when accumulated in the human body (Li et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2018; Liu, Chen, Chen, Liu, & Wang, 2007). Therefore, the removal of chromium(VI) from wastewater is a major concern. Cobalt(II) is another hazardous heavy metal ion that must be removed from aquatic environments. Many industries that rely heavily on cobalt compounds, such as nuclear energy, metallurgy, mining, and electronics industries, introduce cobalt(II) to the environment via wastewater (Acosta-Rodr et al., 2021). Acute cobalt poisoning in humans can cause major health complications, including heart, cell, thyroid, and liver damage (Sheikh, 2016). As understanding of the health issues associated with chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) improves, research into strategies for effectively removing these heavy metal ions from wastewater increases (Bayuo, 2021; Mahey, Kumar, Sharma, Kumar, & Bhardwaj, 2020).

Heavy metal ions are often detected using sophisticated analytical techniques such as atomic adsorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and voltammetry (Jin et al., 2020). These methods are time-consuming, expensive, and require highly skilled personnel (de Silva, McCaughan, McKinney, & Querol, 2003; Junaid, Batool, Harun, Akhter, & Shabbir, 2022). Recently, fluorescent sensors have been proposed as viable alternatives due to their simplicity, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, high spatial resolution, and ability for real-time monitoring (De Acha, Elosúa, Corres, & Arregui, 2019; Dean & Palmer, 2014; Wang, Li, Cheng, Cao, & Liu, 2014; Yao, Chi, Zhou, & Huang, 2014). Fluorescent metal nanoclusters (Shang, Dong, & Nienhaus, 2011), organic dyes (Yu et al., 2019), semiconductor quantum dots (Wu, Hou, Xu, & Chen, 2016), and metal organic frameworks are among the most extensively utilized fluorescent sensors (Zhou et al., 2015); however, these materials have several disadvantages, including unstable photoluminescence and high prices, and they tend to be environmentally unfriendly and cytotoxic. Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) have piqued the interest of researchers due to their high fluorescence intensity (Huang et al., 2013), excellent photoluminescence stability (Yan et al., 2019), tunable fluorescence (Purbia & Paria, 2016), biocompatibility (Jiao et al., 2019), and low toxicity (Xu et al., 2013). Several ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ approaches to C-dot synthesis have been developed over the last few decades (Lin et al., 2015; Pu et al., 2020; Zhou, Sheng, Han, Zou, & Li, 2012; Zhu et al., 2017), including arc-discharge (Xu et al., 2004), laser ablation (Gonçalves, Jorge, Fernandes, & Esteves da Silva, 2010), electrochemical oxidation (Dong et al., 2010), microwaving (Xiao, Yuan, He, & Gao, 2013), and solvothermal treatment (Shen, Wang, Guo, Kuang, & Cao, 2018). As such, C-dots with different sizes, structures, and excitation wavelengths, which in turn affect their fluorescence emission intensity (Pal, Sk, & Chattopadhyay, 2020), can be obtained from various carbon sources. However, poor quantum yield (QY) and few surface functional groups negatively impact C-dot fluorescence and restrict their practical applications (Lin, Luo, Tsai, Wang, & Chen, 2018). One method of improving the fluorescent properties of C-dots is by doping them with heteroatoms, which may expand their uses by increasing the intrinsic activity of their active sites (Cheng et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022; Manioudakis et al., 2019). C-dots are sometimes doped with nitrogen atoms (Chang et al., 2022; Ganiga & Cyriac, 2016), which increases the number of electrons excited from the ground state to the excited state and affects C-dot energy level structure and composition, increasing the photoluminescence intensity (Chang et al., 2022; Ganiga & Cyriac, 2016). C-dots can be used to detect heavy metal ions via fluorescence quenching. Heavy metal ions form complexes with C-dots and excited electrons lose energy and are transferred to the emission state, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Batool et al., 2020). Therefore, synthesizing C-dots from carbon sources containing large quantities of nitrogen is one approach to obtaining C-dots with high QY, the quenching of which is excellent for detecting heavy metal ions in wastewater.

Several methods have been used for the removal of heavy metals ion from wastewater, such as membrane filtration, electrolysis, and ion exchange (Nadaroglu & Kalkan, 2012; Velusamy, Roy, Sundaram, & Kumar Mallick, 2021). However, similar to heavy metal ion detection, these methods require the use of expensive equipment and chemicals, which is highly impractical for large scale applications (Maneechakr & Karnjanakom, 2019). Adsorption is an alternative technique that has more practical applications (Aji, Wiguna, Karunawan, Wati, & Sulhadi, 2017). Several studies on different types of sorbents for the removal of heavy metals ion have been published. Due to its high adsorption efficiency, activated carbon is the most often used sorbent (Aji et al., 2017); however, its synthesis is relatively expensive and demands harsh reaction conditions. Another interesting research area is the utilization of nanomaterials obtained from natural sources such as chitin (Filson & Dawson-Andoh, 2009). Thus far, chitin has shown significant promise as a sorbent and has attracted the interest of researchers (Synowiecki & Al-Khateeb, 2003). Chitin consists of repeating N-acetylglucosamine units that contain large amounts of carbon and nitrogen (Ravi Kumar, 2000); therefore, it is also a promising precursor for nitrogen-doped C-dot synthesis. Furthermore, chitin is biodegradable, chemically and physically stable, cost-efficient, and environmentally friendly (Cui, Yu, & Lau, 2016; Yan & Chen, 2015). Several studies have shown the utility of chitin as a biosorbent for heavy metal ion removal during wastewater treatment (Anastopoulos, Bhatnagar, Bikiaris, & Kyzas, 2017). As such, chitin can be used to remove heavy metals ion discharged in aquatic ecosystems (Anastopoulos et al., 2017; Qasem, Mohammed, & Lawal, 2021). Chitin loads as low as 2 g/L have been shown to effectively remove iron, lead, and zinc ions, and also partially extract cadmium ions from wastewater (Pinto, Al-Abed, & Reisman, 2011). Because of its low cost, stiffness, high aspect ratio, and highly crystalline structure, chitin nanocrystals (ChNCs) are becoming increasingly popular (Lin, Huang, & Dufresne, 2012; Yang, Liu, Pei, Zheng, & Tang, 2020).

We hypothesize that optically active C-dots can be synthesized directly on the surface of ChNCs to create a fluorescent hybrid material can detect and remove heavy metal ions from aqueous media. Due to their excellent optical properties, the fluorescent C-dots can act as detectors for heavy metal ions, which reduce photoluminescence intensity upon adsorption; conversely, ChNC assists in heavy metal ion sorption and their eventual removal from aqueous environments.

We present, for the first time, a novel, inexpensive, effective C-dot/ChNC hybrid smart material for wastewater treatment. This study is important because heavy metal ions are detrimental to human and animal health; therefore, it is imperative that they be removed from water sources. Furthermore, the proposed sorbent has low environmental impact: chitin is a biopolymer, the reaction conditions are mild, and the hybrid material is biodegradable and reusable, making wastewater treatment using this biosorbent an environmentally friendly process.

Chitin from shrimp shell [(С8H13NO5)n, ≥95 %], concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5–38 %, ACS reagent), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ≥99 %), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3, 99 %), copper(II) chloride (CuCl2, 97 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98 %), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3, 98 %), and sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 %) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥99 %), nitric acid (HNO3, 65 %), cobalt(II) chloride (CoCl2, ≥99 %), nickel chloride (NiCl2, ≥98 %), and zinc(II) chloride (ZnCl2, 98 %) were obtained from LenReaktiv. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, ≥98 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥98 %), and potassium nitrate (KNO3, ≥99 %) were purchased from NevaReaktiv. Iron(II) chloride (FeCl2, 99 %) and l-tyrosine (C9H11NO3, 99 %) were purchased from Acros Organics, mercuric nitrate (HgNO3, 98 %) was obtained from Chemmed, boric acid (H3BO3, ≥99 %) was purchased from ReagentPlus, and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 38 %) was purchased from SigmaTek.
Milli-Q-grade deionized water (DI, 18.2 M Ω cm resistivity) was used in all experiments.

Chitin nanocrystals (ChNC) were obtained via acid hydrolysis (Zhang et al., 2015). Chitin from shrimp shell (5 g) was boiled in DI water (150 mL) in a round-bottom flask under reflux at 140 °C for 1 h. Then, concentrated HCl (50 mL) was added dropwise to the reaction vessel and the reaction was stirred (600 rpm) and kept under reflux at 140 °C for 4 h. The reaction was then quenched by pouring the mixture into cold water, and was allowed to sediment overnight. The following morning, the excess supernatant was poured off and the remainder centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was discarded while the sediment was collected and dialyzed against DI water for 2–3 days with the water being changed every 2–3 h. After dialysis, the mixture was sonicated for 30 min, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant collected. The ChNC suspension was refrigerated until further use.

ChNCs (50 mL, 1 wt%), H3BO3 (2 g), and DI water (100 mL) were added to a round-bottom flask and brought to a boil under reflux at 140 °C. Ethylenediamine (5 mL) was added dropwise to the reaction vessel and the mixture stirred at 600 rpm for 10 h under reflux. The reaction was quenched by pouring the mixture into cold water, and was then allowed to sediment overnight. The following morning, the excess supernatant was poured off and the remainder centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was discarded while the sediment was collected and dialyzed against DI water for 12 h with the water being changed every 2–3 h. After dialysis, the suspension was collected and refrigerated until further use.

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential were determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and laser Doppler electrophoresis (LDE), respectively, on a Compact particle size analyzer (Photocor, Russian Federation) at 25 °C in a disposable capillary cell.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted on a VEGA3 scanning electron microscope (Tescan, Czech Republic) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Hitachi HT-7700 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Hitachi High-Technologies, Japan) at 80 kV.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted over an angular range of 2θ = 10°–70° with 0.1° increments on a SmartLab 3 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at the anode. The voltage and current were 30 kV and 30 mA, respectively.

Freeze-dried samples were mixed with crystalline KBr in a 1:5 (w/w) ratio and pressed into pellets. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted on a Nicolet™ Is™ 5 FTIR spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, United States) within the range 4000–400 cm−1 at 4 cm−1 increments. The data was processed using manufacturer software.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a SPECS photoelectron spectrometer (Germany) equipped with a PHOIBOS-150 hemispherical energy analyzer and AlKα irradiation (hν = 1486.6 eV, 150 W). The binding energy (BE) scale was pre-calibrated using the positions of photoelectron of Au4f7/2 (BE = 84.0 eV) and Cu2p3/2 (BE = 932.67 eV) core level peaks. Residual gas pressure exceeded 8 × 10−9. The binding energies of the peaks were calibrated by the position of the C1s peak (BE = 284.7 eV) corresponding to the hydrocarbons presented on the sample surface. For XPS measurements, samples were supported using double-sided conducting copper scotch tape. Spectra analysis and peak fitting were performed with XPSPeak 4.1 software [http://xpspeak.software.informer.com/4.1/].

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse fluorimeter (Agilent Technologies, United States) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy was performed on a Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, United States).

To determine C-dot/ChNC PL quantum yield (QY; PLQY), l-tyrosine was used as a standard. Several l-tyrosine solutions and C-dot/ChNC suspensions with different concentrations were prepared. Their measured absorbances did not exceed 0.1. Then, the integral fluorescence intensity was plotted versus the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum, and C-dot/ChNC QY was determined using the following equation (Qian, Ma, Shan, Feng, & Shao, 2014):

The quenching abilities of various heavy metal ions on C-dot/ChNC fluorescence were investigated. Each ion was obtained at a concentration of 1000 ppm (from their respective salts) before being introduced to a C-dot/ChNC suspension. The metal ion solution (300 μL) was added dropwise to 3 mL of C-dot/ChNC suspension and mixed thoroughly. The quenching ability of the mixed solutions was tested under UV irradiation (λex < 380 nm) and the PL intensity was assessed to determine which ions quenched C-dot/ChNC fluorescence.

DI water was used to prepare several chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) solutions with various concentrations (0 to 1000 ppm). C-dot/ChNC (500 μL) was added to the chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) solutions (5 mL) and stirred at 25 °C for 1 h. The suspension was then centrifuged for 15 min at 6000 rpm and the supernatant collected. The heavy metal ion concentration after adsorption was determined using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The absorbance spectra of chromium(VI) and cobalt(II) were measured at 580 and 510 nm, respectively. C-dot/ChNC adsorption capacity was determined using the following equation (Ulatowska & Stala, 2021):

C-dot/ChNC adsorption ability was measured at pH 1–13 and between 25 and 60 °C to determine the effects of pH and temperature on adsorption capacity.
The data in this study was examined using the Freundlich, Langmuir, and Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherm models.

The Langmuir isotherm model was used to calculate the maximum adsorption capacity (Ayawei, Ebelegi, & Wankasi, 2017). The Langmuir isotherm model can be expressed as:

The 100 ppm Cr(VI) solution was used to determine C-dot/ChNC cyclability. To do so, 5 mL C-dot/ChNC was placed in a Petri dish, then 1 mL Cr(VI) 100 ppm was added dropwise and C-dot/ChNC fluorescence was monitored until it was completely quenched. Then, 15 % (w/v) NaOH was added dropwise to the Petri dish until C-dot/ChNC fluorescence was reignited (Navrotskaya et al., 2022; Zeng, Hu, Peng, Deng, & Liu, 2021). The PL intensity was then measured. The process was repeated until a significant decrease in PL intensity was observed.

As shown in Fig. 1 , ethylenediamine and boric acid were reacted with ChNC under reflux to obtain C-dot/ChNC hybrid nanoparticles. C-dots were synthesized directly on the ChNC surface to prevent C-dot agglomeration and flocculation, and to ensure homogeneity in the C-dot/ChNC nanostructure. This approach also reduces chemical consumption. In this synthesis, ChNCs were obtained via the hydrochloric acid hydrolysis of chitin from shrimp shell, which separated the amorphous and crystalline regions of chitin to produce chitin nanocrystals (Hu, Liu, Lei, Zheng, & Xiao, 2015). ChNC acted as both a carbon source for C-dots as well as a supporting matrix, while ethylenediamine and boric acid acted as a nitrogen source and catalyst, respectively (Jana, Ganguly, Chandrakumar, Rao, & Pal, 2017; Le, Trogen, Varley, Hummel, & Byrne, 2021). During the reaction, hydrolyzed sugar moieties on the ChNC surface reacted with ethylenediamine and acted as nucleation points (Xu et al., 2018). Boric acid allowed C-dot/ChNC to be formed with high efficiency.

Both pure ChNC and hybrid C-dot/ChNC nanoparticles were characterized. SEM analysis showed that the morphologies of ChNC and C-dot/ChNC were very similar and that both were rod-shaped (Fig. 2a and b). TEM was employed to determine the size and morphology of the C-dots on the ChNC surface. Fig. 2c shows that the spherical C-dots had an average size of approximately 5 nm. DLS showed that the ChNCs were approximately 300 nm in length (Fig. S1, Supplementary Data) while TEM analysis (Fig. 2) showed that the nanocrystals were 40–50 nm wide and 400–500 nm long before and after hybrid synthesis. The zeta potential of the ChNC suspension was strongly positive and was determined to be 43 ± 5 mV using laser Doppler electrophoresis (LDE). Chitin is the only positively charged naturally occurring polysaccharide due to the presence of N-amino-d-glucosamine units in its backbone (Roy, Salaün, Giraud, & Ferri, 2017).

XRD analysis was used to assess the crystalline structures of ChNC and C-dot/ChNC. As shown in Fig. 3a (black), the main diffraction peaks of ChNC were observed at 12°, 20°, 22.5°, 27°, and 39°, which corresponded to the (020), (110), (120), (130), and (013) reflection indices of the crystalline planes of chitin, respectively (Jang, Kong, Jeong, Lee, & Nah, 2004; Sikorski, Hori, & Wada, 2009). It should be noted that the C-dot/ChNC XRD pattern (Fig. 3a, blue) indicated that synthesizing C-dots directly on the ChNC surface affected the crystalline structure. As shown in the figure, a decrease in peak intensity was accompanied by the disappearance of distinct peaks. The Debye–Scherrer formula (Ciesla, Stucky, & Schüth, 1998) was used to determine crystallite sizes from the XRD data, and the results correlated well with the TEM data. FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate C-dot/ChNC and ChNC functional groups, and both FTIR spectra showed bands characteristic of chitin (Fig. 3b). The band at 1081–1071 cm−1 was attributed to COC or CO bonds while the band at 1714–1708 cm−1 was assigned to CO. The bands at 3450–3000 cm−1 were attributed to –OH and –NH vibrations (Sikorski et al., 2009; Yi et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 3b, the broad peak at ∼2900 cm−1 is more pronounced in the C-dot/ChNC spectrum (blue), indicating the presence of more –NH groups.

XPS was used to determine C-dot/ChNC composition and surface functional groups (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a (black) confirms the presence of C, O, N in C-dot/ChNC. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b, bottom) shows that carbon exists on the material surface in three states: the peak at 284.7 eV was attributed to CC/CH bonds, the signal at 286.2 eV indicated the presence of C atoms bound to O or N atoms (COC/CN) (Chen et al., 2022; C. Wang et al., 2021), and carbonyl/carboxyl C atoms were observed at 287.7 eV. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c, bottom) shows that nitrogen exists on the material surface in two states: the peak at 339.2 eV was attributed to N atoms bound to C atoms (CN) (Srivastava & Majumder, 2008) and the signal at 401.1 eV is due to N atoms in NH groups. These results confirmed the presence of the functional groups indicated in the FITR spectra.

The PL spectrum of C-dot/ChNC was measured, and the excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 365 and 460 nm, respectively (Fig. 5a). As shown in the inset of Fig. 5a, the hybrid has bright blue fluorescence. The two most important factors to consider for C-dot/ChNC application are PL intensity and optical stability; thus, it is necessary to determine C-dot/ChNC PLQY. The PLQY of a material is the number of photons emitted expressed as a fraction of the number of photons absorbed (Deng et al., 2020). Using l-tyrosine as a reference (Fig. 5b), the PLQY of C-dot/ChNC was determined to be 17 %, which is higher than that of other C-dots reported in the literature (Hoan, Tam, & Pham, 2019; Monte-Filho, Andrade, Lima, & Araujo, 2019; Pudza et al., 2020). This higher PLQY was attributed to nitrogen doping since several studies have shown that nitrogen doping increases C-dot QY (Chang et al., 2022; Ganiga & Cyriac, 2016). Due to the change in electron intensity after nitrogen doping, the number of excited electrons that move to the emission state of C-dot increases, resulting in an increase in QY and PL intensity (Ganiga & Cyriac, 2016; Xu et al., 2013).

Reaction time, ChNC concentration, H3BO3 mass, and ethylenediamine volume were manipulated to optimize reaction conditions in order to obtain highly fluorescent hybrid C-dot/ChNC nanoparticles (Table S1). ChNCs could act as carbon sources for C-dot synthesis because they were receptive to the formation of new functional groups via nitrogen doping, polymerization (crosslinking), and carbonization reactions due to their high specific surface area and surface functional groups (Navrotskaya et al., 2022). Fig. S2 (Supplementary Data) shows that C-dot/ChNC PL intensity dramatically increases in the presence of H3BO3. It should be noted that, while boric acid can act as a co-dopant to nitrogen (Thakur et al., 2022), no significant B-doping was observed in this C-dot/ChNC hybrid. Studies have shown that boric acid can improve luminescence persistence and increase photoluminescence intensity (Jung, Hong, Lee, & Kang, 2005; Yoon et al., 2015); thus, it was assumed that H3BO3 occupied a similar role in this system. As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1 (Supplementary Data), C-dot/ChNC nanoparticles with the brightest fluorescence were obtained at 140 °C in a 10 h reaction, using 25 mL 1.0 wt% ChNC, 0.5 g H3BO3, and 2 mL ethylenediamine. The optimized C-dot/ChNC suspensions were used in subsequent experiments.

The effect of excitation wavelength, pH, time, ionic strength, and UV irradiation on C-dot/ChNC PL intensity were studied (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6a, an excitation wavelength of 365 nm resulted in C-dot/ChNC with the brightest fluorescence. As Fig. 6b shows, C-dot/ChNC PL was strongest at pH 6–7 and PL intensity decreased with increasing and decreasing pH. Fig. 6c and d show that PL intensity remained stable over a 30-day refrigeration period, regardless of pH. The effect of ionic strength on PL intensity was investigated using increasing aqueous NaCl concentration and, as shown in Fig. 6e, ionic strength has minimal effect on C-dot/ChNC PL intensity. To determine the effect of continual UV irradiation on PL intensity, the sample was irradiated for 24 h. The results show that prolonged exposure to UV irradiation had negligible effect on PL intensity (Fig. 6f).

The ability of C-dot/ChNC to detect various heavy metal ions was studied. The quenching abilities of Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ag+, K+, Na+, Cr6+, Zn2+, and Hg2+ ions on C-dot/ChNC fluorescence were investigated. Fig. 7a shows that Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+ and Cr6+ noticeably reduced C-dot/ChNC PL intensity. Although the hybrid shows good detectability for Fe2+ and Fe3+, these ions are easily detected (they form complexes that give water a reddish tinge) and removed from wastewater via simple methods such as chemical oxidation, shock chlorination, phosphate treatment, water softeners, and iron removal filters (Aragaw, Bogale, & Aragaw, 2021). As previously stated, both cobalt(II) and chromium(VI) have complex, expensive, and highly technical detection and removal methods; therefore, Co2+ and Cr6+ were used for the remainder of the work. Fig. 7b and c show digital images and the PL spectrum of C-dot/ChNC quenched by Co2+ (red) and Cr6+ (green) ions. Fig. 7d illustrates a potential mechanism of C-dot/ChNC heavy metal ion detection. The quenching of C-dot/ChNC fluorescence is due to the formation of complexes between C-dot/ChNC functional groups containing –OH, –COOH, –CO and heavy metal ions. When C-dot/ChNCs have multiple functional groups containing nitrogen, these molecules can transfer their lone pair of electrons to the heavy metal ion, filling the d-orbitals of the metal ion and creating coordination complexes that are non-emitting (Bansod et al., 2017a; Chi et al., 2020; Sun & Lei, 2017). As shown in Fig. 7e, complete quenching occurred within 30 min.

C-dot/ChNC also shows high selectivity for Cr6+ and Co2+ ions. Fig. S4a and b (Supplementary data) show the selectivity of C-dot/ChNC for Cr6+ and Co2+, respectively, in the presence of other heavy metals ions. With high selectivity, C-dot/ChNC shows enormous potential for detecting Cr6+ and Co2+ ion in practical applications.

The Co2+ and Cr6+ adsorption ability of C-dot/ChNC were studied using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The Co2+ adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC was directly determined using a calibration curve. Cobalt(II) has a maximum absorbance wavelength (λ
max ) of 510 nm (Acosta-rodr et al., 2021; Nadaroglu & Kalkan, 2012) (Fig. S5a, Supplementary data), which is distinct from that of C-dot/ChNC (λ
max = 365 nm); therefore, Co2+ adsorption was measured at 510 nm and absorbance was plotted against Co2+ concentration (Fig. S5b, Supplementary data). This linear plot was used to determine Co2+ concentration before and after adsorption, and the Co2+ adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC was calculated using Eq. 2. However, the Cr6+ adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC was indirectly determined. This is because chromium(VI) has a λ
max of 350 nm (Fig. S5c, Supplementary Data) (Sheikhi & Rezaei, 2021), which is close to that of C-dot/ChNC. Thus, Cr6+ was converted into Cr3+ according to the reaction (Eq. 8) (Zhao et al., 2017):
(8)
K 2
Cr 2
O 7
+ 3
Na 2
SO 3
+ 8 HCl → 2 Cr
Cl 3
+ 3
Na 2
SO 4
+ 4
H 2
O + 2 KCl

Cr3+ has a λ
max of 580 nm (Choi, Lee, Yeong Jae, Joe, & Kim, 2001) and is not adsorbed by C-dot/ChNC in this study. Na2SO3 (5 wt%) and concentrated HCl (36–38 %) were added in excess to ensure that all the Cr6+ ions were converted to Cr3+ ions. After conversion, Cr3+ adsorption was measured at 580 nm and absorbance was plotted against Cr3+ concentration (Fig. S5d, Supplementary Data). The concentration of Cr3+ in the supernatant after adsorption was determined using Eq. 8. Then, the Cr6+ adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC was calculated using Eq. 2.

As shown in Fig. 8a, the adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC for both Co2+ and Cr6+ exceeded 150 mg/g of sorbent. The effects of pH and temperature on C-dot/ChNC adsorbance capacity were investigated. The results show that C-dot/ChNC has the highest adsorption capacity at pH 6–7 and decreases with increasing acidity and basicity (Fig. 8b). These results confirm the dependence of PL intensity on pH, as discussed prior. The results also show that C-dot/ChNC adsorption capacity decreased with increasing temperature (Fig. 8c). This may be because the free movement of the sorbent nucleation points and heavy metal ions increased when a threshold temperature was realized, resulting in bonds between heavy metal ions and functional groups on the C-dot/ChNC surface breaking (Aksu, Tatlı, & Tunç, 2008).

To fully understand the adsorption process, the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin-Radushkevich adsorption isotherms were studied to determine the maximum adsorption capacity of C-dot/ChNC, the adsorption strength, and whether adsorption was physical or chemical in nature (Ayawei et al., 2017). Isothermal adsorption models are mathematical models that depict the distribution of adsorbates (liquid phase) and sorbents (solid phase) based on assumptions about the heterogeneity and homogeneity of solid surfaces as well as the interoperability of adsorbed substances (Theydan, 2018). Monolayer adsorption takes place at certain homogeneous sites on the adsorbent. In such cases, the Langmuir equation is typically used.

The Freundlich model is an empirical equation based on material adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces and can be used to determine adsorption strength (Bhatt & Shah, 2015; Vasiliu et al., 2011). The Freundlich models for C-dot/ChNC Cr6+ and Co2+ are presented in Fig. S6c and d (Supplementary Data), respectively. The adsorption strengths, calculated using Eq. 4, were 0.26 for Cr6+ and 0.39 for Co2+, confirming the high uniformity of the C-dot/ChNC surface and demonstrating the favorable adsorption of the indicated heavy metal ions (Yahaya Pudza et al., 2020). The closer
1 n
is to 1, the stronger the adsorption; therefore, Cr6+ and Co2+ are weakly adsorbed on the C-dot/ChNC surface.

The average adsorption energy (E) indicates the nature of the adsorption process. When E < 8 kJ/mol, the adsorption is physical in nature while adsorption is chemical in nature for E = 8–16 kJ/mol (Bhatt & Shah, 2015; He et al., 2013). Fig. S6e and f (Supplementary Data) show the Dubinin-Radushkevich models for Cr6+ and Co2+, respectively. Eq. 7 was used to determine that E = 8.90 for Cr6+ and E = 8.75 for Co2+, indicating that their adsorption is chemical in nature. Table 1 summarizes the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm results of C-dot/ChNC Cr6+ and Co2+ adsorbance.

XPS analysis was used to investigate the structure of C-dot/ChNC before and after Cr6+ adsorption (Fig. 4). Fig. 4a shows the presence of Cr (red) in addition to C, O, N atoms. Fig. 4b and c shows a decrease in peak intensity for the indicated functional groups on the C-dot/ChNC surface before (top) and after (bottom) Cr6+ adsorption. These results indicate bond formation between functional groups on the C-dot/ChNC surface and heavy metal ions after adsorption, suggesting that it is chemical in nature.

One of the most important properties of both sorbents and heavy metal ion detectors is their cyclability (Yeo et al., 2020). Reusable systems are more cost-effective and environmentally friendly. A defining factor of sorbent and heavy metal ion detector cyclability is regeneration, which is defined by the return of a sorbent to its initial state and can be achieved through the desorption of heavy metal ions from the sorbent surface. The subsequent desorption of heavy metals ions from the sorbent surface is necessary for assessing the cyclability of the sorbent. It should be noted that while chemisorption is generally irreversible, conducive reaction conditions, such as high energy of regeneration, can allow some of the adsorbent to be removed from the sorbent surface (Kwon et al., 2011). Typically, desorbents such as acids, alkalis, and alcohols are employed during heavy metal ion desorption (Chatterjee & Abraham, 2019). With the notable exception of Cr6+ ions, almost all heavy metal ions are desorbed using inorganic acids (e.g., HCl, HNO3 and H2SO4) while ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid is used to remove Pb2+ and Zn2+ (Chatterjee & Abraham, 2019; Mishra, 2014). Since it is usually present in its anionic form (Cr2O7 2−), Cr6+ is often desorbed using bases (e.g., NaOH, Na2CO3, or NaHCO3) (Chatterjee & Abraham, 2019; Mishra, 2014).

In this study, C-dot/ChNC cyclability was studied using Cr6+ desorption. Cr6+ was adsorbed on the C-dot/ChNC surface then desorbed using concentrated NaOH. C-dot/ChNC was completely regenerated within 10 min of fluorescence quenching (Fig. 9 ). Video S1 (Supplementary Data) shows the quenching and regeneration process, which was repeated until a dramatic decrease in PL intensity was observed. As shown in Fig. S7 (Supplementary Data), PL intensity drastically decreased after seven regenerations.

In conclusion, we created a reusable, inexpensive, effective fluorescent C-dot/ChNC hybrid sorbent for the simultaneous detection and removal of chromium(IV) and cobalt(II) ions from aqueous environments. The hybrid sorbent, which had a QY of 17 %, was stable for a minimum of 30 days and the fluorescence was strongest at pH 6–7. The hybrid showed high selectivity for Cr6+ and Co2+ ions and had a maximum adsorption capacity of 155 and 152 mg/g for Cr6+ and Co2+ ions, respectively. C-dot/ChNC had an adsorption strength of 0.26 for Cr6+ and 0.39 for Co2+, indicating that the heavy metal ions are weakly adsorbed on the C-dot/ChNC surface and can easily be removed using an acid or base for sorbent regeneration, suggesting high cyclability. Quenching and regeneration studies showed that the hybrid had a lifetime of seven cycles.
